basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Ṭāriq — “The Night Star,” “the one that knocks” — names the star that appears at night, piercing enough to pierce the darkness. The oath opens a dense sūra of seventeen verses: by the heaven and by the night star… every soul has a guardian set over it. Then comes the invitation to turn upon oneself: let man consider then of what he was created — of a mere spurt of water; and He who drew man from so little can, all the more, bring him back. On the Day when secrets are put to the test, none will have either force or defender. Qushayrī, on the first verse, opens the spiritual path: the piercing star is not only physical — they are also the stars of gnosis (nujūm al-maʿrifa) that point to the One, and whose light guides the people of insight (ulū al-baṣāʾir). The sūra closes on a word of astonishing serenity: they plot, I conduct; give them yet a little respite. The heavens are not in a hurry.

versets.trad: By the heaven and by the night star!
versets.commentaire: al-ṭāriq (الطَّارِق): that which comes by night. The root ṭ-r-q names the knock at the door — the arrival of an unexpected visitor. A star that knocks against the roof of heaven. Qushayrī: “He swears by the heaven, and by the star that knocks by night.” Then he opens the inward reading: “It is also said that they are the stars of gnosis (nujūm al-maʿrifa, نُجُوم المَعْرِفَة) which point to Unicity, and from whose light the people of insight (ulū al-baṣāʾir, أُولُو البَصَائِر) draw their direction.” Every true knowing is a knock struck at the night of the heart.

mots.def: ṭāriq: that which comes by night, that knocks at the door; here, the star that appears in the night. Active participle of ṭaraqa.

versets.trad: But what will let you know what the night star is?
versets.commentaire: A rhetorical question of amplification — mā adrāka, already met in sūra 97. What follows does not define the thing; it magnifies its scope.

mots.def: adrā: to make known; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is the star whose light pierces the night.
versets.commentaire: al-najm al-thāqib (النَّجْم الثَّاقِب): Qushayrī, in a few words: “the luminous, the lofty.” The root th-q-b says piercing: the star whose light traverses the darkness. It is the very image of divine revelation: that which pierces, which forces a path, which manifests itself in the night of the world. And on another level, it is also the image of the true sage: his radiance traverses the veils that the night of the heart had set.

mots.def: thāqib: active participle of thaqaba; that pierces, that traverses with its light.

versets.ar: إِن كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَّمَّا عَلَيْهَا حَافِظٌ
versets.tr: in kullu nafsin lammā ʿalayhā ḥāfiẓ
versets.trad: For every soul there is a guardian set over it.
versets.commentaire: Response of the oath. There is no soul that does not have a guardian set over it. Qushayrī specifies: “a guardian among the angels, who keeps watch over its works, its sustenance and its term.” But he adds the awakening sense: “and who urges it to a continual wakefulness (tayaqquẓ, تَيَقُّظ) and a beautiful keeping of itself (taḥaffuẓ, تَحَفُّظ).” To have a guardian is also to feel, in the very night, a watching gaze: not a threatening one, but that of an unseen companion who awakens to attention.

mots.def: negation particle with restrictive value paired with lammā.
restrictive particle: there is none save.
ḥāfiẓ: active participle of ḥafiẓa; guardian, preserver.

versets.trad: Let man consider then of what he was created!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “He urges him to reflect and to reason, until he knows the perfection of His power, His knowledge, and His will — glory be to Him.” Before looking at the sky, look at yourself. Before arguing about God, examine your own beginning. True spirituality does not begin with abstractions; it begins with the precise remembrance of what one came from.

mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: He was created of a spurting water
versets.commentaire: māʾ dāfiq: gushing water, semen. The verse is unafraid of materiality: it states, without circumlocution, the humblest of origins. And it is precisely the humility of that origin that makes the greatness of the work visible. From so little, a man.

mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: dāfiq: active participle of dafaqa; that gushes forth.

versets.trad: issuing from between the loins and the ribs.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “it issues from the loins of the father and the breastbone of the mother.” Begetting involves two bodies, two precise origins. The verse honours this precision to remind us that nothing in man came of itself: all bears the mark of a dialogue that precedes him.

mots.def: bayn: between; in the construct genitive.
ṣulb: loins, spine; seat of procreation in the male according to ancient anatomy.
tarāʾib: plural of tarība; the ribs, the breastbone; anatomical seat in the female.

versets.trad: Truly, God has the power to bring him back
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī formulates the great argument: “Over his return to life, and his second creation, He is All-powerful — for He has power over perfection. And power over a thing entails power over its like. Repetition belongs to the same register as the beginning.” If God could create once from a single spurt, He can recreate from what remains of it. From the standpoint of divine power, resurrection is but a second time.

mots.def: rajʿ: return, bringing back; with suffix pronoun.
qādir: active participle of qadara; able, powerful.

versets.trad: on the Day when secrets are unveiled.
versets.commentaire: An overwhelming verse. al-sarāʾir (السَّرَائِر): plural of sarīra, intimate secret. tublā (تُبْلَى): will be put to the test, tried (same root as balāʾ, trial). Qushayrī, in one word: “the Day when the consciences (al-ḍamāʾir, الضَّمَائِر) are put to the test.” The Day, then, is not only the judgment of acts — it is the examination of what the acts meant to the one performing them. All that was buried (intentions, motives, half-knowings) will be placed in the crucible. The verse already proposes a discipline: prepare each day this sarīra to bear the trial.

mots.def: yawm: day; in the adverbial accusative.
balā: to test, to try; in the passive imperfect.
mots.gram: passive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: sarāʾir: plural of sarīra; intimate secrets, what the soul hides.

versets.ar: فَمَا لَهُ مِن قُوَّةٍ وَلَا نَاصِرٍ
versets.tr: fa-mā lahu min quwwatin wa-lā nāṣir
versets.trad: Man will then have neither force, nor defender.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “that man, on that day, will have no helper to ward off the decree of God.” At the moment when the secrets come out, no outer advocate any more; no inner force either. The verse does not humiliate; it prepares. What will not be carried by oneself on the Day must be held today.

mots.def: nāṣir: active participle of naṣara; helper, defender.

versets.trad: By the heaven that fulfils its returns!
versets.commentaire: A second cycle of oaths. dhāt al-rajʿ: “endowed with return.” Qushayrī, in a word: “that is, the rain.” The heaven returns to the earth in the rain it pours back. A splendid image: what rises (the vapour) returns in the form of what gives life. The heaven is not only elevation — it is also that which gives itself back.

mots.def: rajʿ: return; by extension, the rain that returns to the earth.

versets.trad: By the earth that opens forth!
versets.commentaire: dhāt al-ṣadʿ: “endowed with the cleaving.” Qushayrī: “the cleaving is the splitting of the earth by vegetation, for crops and trees.” A silent image of grace: the earth must open for life to push through. And one hears, behind it, the analogy: the heart too must open.

mots.def: ṣadʿ: fissure, cleft; here, the cleaving by which plants pierce the earth.

versets.trad: Here, truly, is a decisive word,
versets.commentaire: qawl faṣl (قَوْل فَصْل): a word that decides, a decisive word. Qushayrī, in a word: “the Quran is a categorical (jazm, جَزْم) word.” The word faṣl is of the same root as what separates the true from the false. God's word does not float in the indeterminate: it decides. It is this inner precision that gives it its light.

versets.trad: and it is no frivolous discourse.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, soberly: “al-hazl is the opposite of seriousness; the Quran is neither vain nor play.” God's word is set as the opposite of jesting. It is a discreet but crucial distinction: there are words that retract themselves, that qualify themselves, that take back what they say. Not this one. What is said here is said, and stands.

mots.def: hazl: jest, frivolity; opposite of seriousness.

versets.commentaire: kayd (كَيْد): cunning, stratagem. Qushayrī: “they devise a ruse.” The sūra names the resistance to the decisive word: it is not only denial, it is manoeuvre. Men against God invent plans to make the word fail.

mots.def: kāda: to plot, to scheme; imperfect plural.
kayd: stratagem, cunning; in the cognate accusative.

versets.trad: and I devise one as well.
versets.commentaire: A verse to be understood with delicacy. Qushayrī: “They use cunning; and We, We establish a decree by Our act, We tie a creation; and We requite them for their cunning by what We treat them with — istidrāj (drawing on by degrees, اسْتِدْرَاج) and respite (imhāl).” The divine kayd is not, then, of the same order as the human one; it is a subtle justice that lets them go on thinking they are winning, until the moment they discover they have trapped themselves. The grace, here too, lies in the apparent patience.

mots.def: kāda in the imperfect 1st pers. sing.

versets.trad: So grant the disbelievers a respite, grant them a little while!
versets.commentaire: The sūra, which seemed ready to thunder, closes in an astonishingly gentle gesture. mahhil… amhilhum ruwaydā: give them a delay, give them time, gently. The very tripling of the verb (mahhil intensive, amhil redoubled, ruwayd diminutive) slows the rhythm. Qushayrī, in a word: “look upon them, give them a little more, and go slowly with them.” This is the Most High's instruction to the Prophet: do not be in a hurry. Haste is not a prophetic virtue; it is the opposite of the divine mirṣād (cf. 89:14), which knows how to wait. And for the one who preaches or teaches, it is a precious word: give each one the time he needs.

mots.def: mahhala: intensive form of mahala; to grant a delay.
kāfirīn: plural of kāfir; the deniers.
amhala: derived form; to grant a respite; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperative verb + suffix pronoun, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ruwayd: intensive diminutive; gently, a little, without haste.
